channel.title: The Leadership Compass by Sudarshan
channel.description: Bold ideas and honest conversations about HR, leadership, and building workplaces that work for everyone.

item.title: Strong Legs or an Easier Road: What HR Should Really Build
item.description: Every HR team faces the same choice in disguise: smooth the path for people, or build their capacity to walk hard ones. Why the best organizations choose stronger legs.

item.title: Why the Best Leaders Are Unlearning Everything They Know
item.description: The leadership playbook from 2015 is broken. In a world of AI, distributed teams, and five generations in the workforce, the leaders who thrive are the ones brave enough to start over.

item.title: The HR Tech Stack Is a Lie (And What to Build Instead)
item.description: Sixteen tools, zero integration, and a spreadsheet holding it all together. The problem is not technology - it is architecture. A three-phase roadmap for an HR stack that works.

item.title: Psychological Safety Is Not About Being Nice
item.description: The most psychologically safe teams argue more, not less. What the research actually found, the nice-team trap, and four practices that build real safety.

item.title: AI Won't Replace Managers. But Managers Who Use AI Will.
item.description: The real divide is between managers who treat AI as a capability and those who pretend it is not happening.

item.title: The Quiet Power of Leaders Who Listen More Than They Talk
item.description: In a culture that rewards the loudest voice, the most effective leaders master strategic silence.

item.title: Stop Hiring for Culture Fit. Start Hiring for Culture Add.
item.description: Culture fit became a bias laundromat. A practical framework for hiring people who add what is missing.

item.title: Your Leadership Brand Is What Happens When You Leave the Room
item.description: Reputation is built in the micro-moments your team experiences every day.

item.title: Performance Reviews Are Dead. Long Live Continuous Feedback.
item.description: Annual reviews are a relic. What the companies winning the talent war replaced them with.

item.title: How to Build a Culture Deck That Isn't Performative Nonsense
item.description: Most culture decks are aspirational fiction. How to write one that actually reflects reality.

item.title: Your Calendar Is Your Real Strategy
item.description: Strategy decks describe intentions. Calendars describe reality. How to audit the only strategy your organization actually has.

item.title: Exit Interviews Are Too Late. Try Stay Interviews.
item.description: By the time someone is honest in an exit interview, the insight costs you a rehire. Five questions that collect the same intelligence while you can still act.

item.title: Your Meetings Are Your Culture
item.description: Forget the values poster. The way your meetings run is the most accurate description of your culture that exists - and the highest-leverage place to change it.

item.title: Five Generations, One Workplace
item.description: The widest age range any workforce has ever contained - and most of what we believe about generational differences is wrong. What actually differs matters more.

item.title: Your Best People Leave Quietly. Your Worst Stay Loudly.
item.description: Top performers rarely complain on the way out - they just leave. Why retention systems miss the people who matter most, and the quiet signals to watch.

item.title: The First 90 Days: Listen Before You Touch Anything
item.description: New leaders feel pressure to make their mark fast. The ones who last spend their first quarter learning the system before they change it.

item.title: Delegation Is Not Dumping
item.description: Handing someone a task is easy. Handing them an outcome - with the authority, context, and tolerance for difference it requires - is the real skill.

item.title: The Courage to Be Disliked: Decisions That Cost Popularity
item.description: Every consequential leadership decision creates losers. Leaders who need to be liked end up making the one choice that pleases no one: avoidance.

item.title: Feedback Is a Gift Nobody Wraps Well
item.description: We have sandwiched, softened, and acronym-ed feedback into uselessness. What actually helps: specific observation, clean delivery, and genuine curiosity.

item.title: Leading Through Uncertainty Without Faking Certainty
item.description: Teams do not need leaders who know the future. They need leaders who can be honest about the fog and decisive inside it - at the same time.

item.title: The Succession Question Every Leader Avoids
item.description: If you disappeared for ninety days, what would break? Succession is not a someday project for the board - it is a weekly leadership discipline.

item.title: Energy, Not Time, Is Your Scarcest Resource
item.description: Two hours of focused energy outproduce eight hours of depletion. Why leaders should manage their energy portfolio as deliberately as their calendar.

item.title: People Analytics Without the Creepiness
item.description: The line between insight and surveillance is thinner than most dashboards admit. How to build a people analytics practice your employees would endorse.

item.title: The Skills-Based Organization: Promise vs. Plumbing
item.description: Everyone wants to hire and deploy by skills instead of pedigree. Almost nobody has the plumbing: taxonomies, verification, and managers who trust it.

item.title: Compensation Transparency Is Coming. Get Ahead of It.
item.description: Between legislation and Glassdoor, pay secrecy is already dead - it just has not been buried. The organizations that thrive will fix their pay architecture before showing it.

item.title: Workforce Planning in an Age You Can't Predict
item.description: Five-year headcount plans are fiction now. The replacement is not better forecasting - it is building a workforce that can bend without breaking.

item.title: HR Business Partners: From Complaint Desk to Strategy Table
item.description: The HRBP role was designed to be strategic and is mostly consumed by escalations and admin. What it takes - from the partner and the function - to claim the seat.

item.title: The Onboarding Window: 90 Days That Decide Three Years
item.description: New hires decide early whether they made a mistake - and the decision sticks. Why onboarding is a retention system disguised as paperwork week.

item.title: Policies Are Not Culture: When to Write Rules and When Not To
item.description: Every policy is a monument to a moment someone was not trusted. When rules genuinely protect people - and when they just outsource judgment.

item.title: Recognition Is a System, Not a Mood
item.description: Left to mood and memory, recognition flows to the visible and the recent. How to build appreciation into the operating rhythm so nobody great goes unseen.

item.title: Remote Culture Isn't Built on Zoom Happy Hours
item.description: Forced fun is not culture. Remote culture lives in how work actually flows: documentation, decision visibility, async respect, and deliberate moments of realness.

item.title: The Silence Audit: Hearing What Your Culture Won't Say
item.description: Every culture has topics that conversations bend around. Learning to detect the silences - and what it costs you not to.

item.title: Conflict Is a Feature: Building Teams That Argue Well
item.description: Teams that never fight are not healthy - they are quiet. The skill is not eliminating conflict but building the container that makes it productive.

item.title: Rituals Beat Values: Small Repeated Acts That Define Teams
item.description: Values describe intentions. Rituals install behavior. Why the smallest repeated practices - demo days, gratitude rounds, failure shares - out-build any poster.

item.title: When Culture Eats Your Best Intentions: Change That Sticks
item.description: Most culture change initiatives die quietly within a year - eaten by the incentives, habits, and power structures they ignored. What the survivors do differently.

item.title: Trust Is Built in Drops and Lost in Buckets
item.description: Trust is the operating capital of every team - accumulated slowly through kept promises, spent suddenly through one bad moment. The maintenance schedule matters.

item.title: The Four-Day Week: What the Trials Actually Show
item.description: Beyond the headlines: what four-day week pilots consistently find, what they quietly gloss over, and how to know if your organization is a candidate.

item.title: Hybrid Is Hard Mode: Why Half-Remote Beats You Up
item.description: Fully remote has playbooks. Fully in-office has tradition. Hybrid - the compromise everyone chose - quietly demands more design than either extreme.

item.title: Skills Have Half-Lives Now: Learning as Infrastructure
item.description: When technical skills decay in a few years, learning cannot be an annual training budget. It has to be plumbing: time, proximity to work, and proof it counts.

item.title: The Gig Inside: Internal Talent Marketplaces
item.description: Project-based internal gigs promise fluid talent and faster development. The technology is the easy part - the hoarding managers are not.

item.title: Automation Anxiety: Leading People Who Fear the Roadmap
item.description: Your roadmap says efficiency. Your people hear replacement. How to lead automation honestly - without false promises or corrosive silence.

item.title: The Office Is a Tool, Not a Default
item.description: The question was never office versus remote. It is: what is an office for, when presence is no longer the proxy for work?

item.title: Boundaries in an Always-On Workplace
item.description: The technology that freed work from the office also freed it from the evening, the weekend, and the holiday. Boundaries are now infrastructure leaders must build.

item.title: Job Descriptions Are Lying to Your Candidates
item.description: Wish-list requirements, vague responsibilities, and a tone nobody speaks - the standard job description repels the people it should attract. Write the honest version.

item.title: The Interview Is a Sample, Not a Ceremony
item.description: Unstructured interviews mostly measure confidence and chemistry. The fix is treating every hour as a work sample - structured, job-shaped, and scored before the debrief.

item.title: Internal Mobility: Your Best Hire Already Works for You
item.description: Proven, cultured, and pre-vetted - internal candidates outperform external hires on most measures. So why does your process favor strangers?

item.title: References, Reimagined: Questions That Actually Predict
item.description: The standard reference call is a pleasant formality with near-zero information. Asked differently, references become the highest-signal hour in your process.

item.title: The Counteroffer Trap
item.description: The panicked counteroffer feels like retention. It is usually an expensive postponement that teaches your team the only way to get a raise is a rival offer.

item.title: Hiring Slow Isn't a Virtue: Speed With Discipline
item.description: "Hire slow, fire fast" confused thoroughness with duration. Great candidates are gone in weeks; most process length is queueing, not rigor.

item.title: Potential Over Polish: Spotting High-Ceiling Candidates
item.description: Polish is what privilege and practice look like in an interview. Potential is what compounds after you hire. Learning to tell them apart is a competitive edge.
